Title: How America Works... and Why it Doesn't: A Brief Guide to the US Political System
Author: William           Cooper
My Review:
William Cooper’s How America Works... and Why it Doesn't is a smart, straightforward guide to the U.S. political system. It does a great job explaining why the government works the way it does—and why it often doesn’t. The book is clear and easy to follow, making it a great pick to understand how things like gridlock, polarization, and campaign finance issues affect the system.

Cooper gives a lot of insight into the flaws without being too pessimistic, and he offers some ideas for fixing things. That said, I wish it went deeper into the solutions—it leans more on the “why things are broken” side than the “how to fix them” side.

Still, it’s a solid read if you’re interested in politics or want to understand the mess we’re in. It's worth picking up.

Title: Breaking the Cycle: the 6 Stages of Healing from Childhood Family Trauma: Support and Recovery for What You Were Once Powerless to Change
Author: Kaytlyn Gillis
My Review:
This book helped me feel validated in the way that my family structure influenced my entire being. What is the best part of about feeling validated in how I was affected by abuse, and how I let such toxicity and trauma perpetuate in my life to this day gives me the power to change not only the way it affected me but also my boundaries so I do not have to feel obligated to others who are toxic and abusive.

The other day I felt like I had to explain my growth rather than admire my wisdom and not need validation from others. The book has helped me find strength in not having to apologise for who I am. It has helped me find more strength in leaving my abusive marriage, to embrace the pain and trauma rather than perpetuate it by staying in it. If you have ever felt like your family structure has had negative effects on your life, I highly recommend this book as a great way to start making choices for yourself, separate of the family structure you were subjected to. (I am an empath and was the scapegoat in my family.)

My Review: I think it is important to note that this book was written by a patient, not a doctor. However, it is an excellent high level explanation and reminder about the illness. Due to the ease of digestion, I do recommend reading this book if you are just starting out learning about the illness, whether you are a patient or your loved one is. It is a great first step in feeling empowered over a often misunderstood ailment.

Title: You Sound Like a White Girl: The Case for Rejecting Assimilation
Author: Julissa  Arce
My Review: This book was extremely enlightening. As a half-Mayan woman who was raised primarily in my Dutch roots, it reminded me of the importance of embracing my Mayan blood. Whether intentional or not, there is a lot of negative self-regard for Hispanic minorities. As I work on changing my career to my passions rather than conventionally successful tech careers, this book reminded me to embrace all parts of me equally. I highly recommend this book, especially for anybody else who may struggle with their identity.

Title: Dear Ijeawele, or A Feminist Manifesto in Fifteen Suggestions
Author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
My Review:
I loved this book. I do not think this book should be read by any demographic in particular. It should be read by mothers, fathers, sons, and daughters. It has great things that we can all think about in the role we play in making the world a place with more equitability for all. Some of my favourite points:

1) Never speak of marriage as an achievement

2) Teach your daughter to question language

3) Teach your daughter to enjoy reading (duh)

4) Teach your daughter to be curious

5) Let your daughter's father raise her too. He is not 'helping' he is being a father.

My Review:
While I am reading these books to try to understand my complex trauma, I found this one to be so exceptionally ubiquitous for all aspects and all people. It discusses our pain and suffering and how it affects our romantic relationships. It goes into how to heal that, especially for those who do not have cPTSD or PTSD. However, there were many great pieces that I think individuals (like me) who have PTSD and cPTSD.

I found a lot of this book helpful in explaining so much of my past and my behaviors now and why. It gave me room to be compassionate to myself and my pain. I highly recommend it. My only complaint with this book is that I did not necessarily agree with the way it was laid out. But that's a personal preference.

Title: Reconciliation: Healing the Inner Child
Author: Thich Nhat Hanh
My Review: If you feel like you have any discomfort from childhood, I highly recommend this book. As I am processing my childhood trauma, I found this book to be a combination of both the Buddhist philosophy and the neuroscientific evidence of how meditation and mindfulness can alter our being from the brain outward.  Thich Nhat Hanh was always an amazing monk with an incredible story of his experience during Vietnam War. This book, written with such peace, gives me hope to heal my inner child too.

Author: Max Lugavere

Title: Getting Through What You Can't Get over: Stories, Tips, and Inspiration to Help You Move Past Your Pain into Lasting Freedom
Author: Anita Agers-Brooks
My Review:
Rarely do I read a book out of sheer desperation for knowing or understanding something better. This was, sadly, one of those times. This book just didn’t give me what I am searching for. It isn’t terrible. It did have the aspect of ‘its okay to hurt’ that I am still desperately trying to understand.

I don’t think I can fully fault the book, however. Anybody who’s experience complete loss and grief probably knows that it’s possibly the only time you’ll never find what you need.

Title: World of Wonders: In Praise of Fireflies, Whale Sharks, and Other Astonishments
Author: Aimee Nezhukumatathil
My Review: Such a beautiful tribute to nature and the world around us. The prose was poetic but the memoir reminded me of my own childhood, my own adventures being plucked from home to new places around the world, trying to find ways to fit in, a difficult thing when you're any child, but exacerbated when you are a darker skinned foreigner who speaks differently. Aimee's adventures in her head is something that I think anybody can resonate with in regards to their childhood. Even if you are not into nature (I don't know who isn't) I think everybody can read this book and smile at the connections she makes between her own life and the world of wonders around us.

Title: Negroes with Guns
Author: Robert Franklin Williams
My Review: A very uncomfortable and necessary read. As somebody who grew up with privilege but always appreciated the importance of Civil Rights and the Civil Rights Movement, I had never read of the more violent and uncomfortable truths from those who had to live it. I recommend anybody who cares about what still happens in America to read this book.

Title: King Leopold's Ghost: A Story of Greed, Terror, and Heroism in Colonial Africa
Author: Adam Hochschild
My Review:
It is not for the overly sensitive. However, it does an excellent job of setting up the history of Europe's interference in Africa. The first half of the book is fascinating in the relationships in Europe and its affects on Africa.

However, the second half of the book is a gruesome encounter of the atrocities that were performed in the Congo under Leopold.

It's definitely a cautionary tale on loving your children.

Author: Vivek H. Murthy

Title: A Very Stable Genius: Donald J. Trump's Testing of America
Author: Philip Rucker
My Review:
Disclaimer: I have never supported Trump and cannot stand him for many reasons.

Of the books that I have read about the Trump administration, this one has been the best.

It does not make Trump out to be a villain in the presidency, but rather highlights the dangers of having somebody in that office who simply does not understand the office. It does a great job of describing the difficult job the administration has in gently guiding Trump through the many decisions he does not seem to understand how to make.

What I very much enjoyed about the book (in a bittersweet way) was that it definitely explains why Trump will not be removed from office, for better or for worse. If you want to understand this presidency beyond soundbites and headlines, I highly recommend this read.
